food_name: Butter Naan / Garlic Naan / Butter Roti

local_names.chinese_simplified: 黄油馕 / 蒜香馕 / 黄油印度薄饼
local_names.chinese_traditional: 黃油饢 / 蒜香饢 / 黃油印度薄餅
local_names.malay: Naan Mentega / Naan Bawang Putih / Roti Mentega
local_names.tamil: வெண்ணெய் நான் / பூண்டு நான் / வெண்ணெய் ரொட்டி
local_names.hindi: बटर नान / लहसुन नान / बटर रोटी
local_names.romanized_local: Butter Naan / Garlic Naan / Butter Roti

region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely served in Singapore Indian and Indian-fusion restaurants

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers naan bread generically; no specific butter naan or garlic naan subtype exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers leavened flatbreads broadly; no butter naan or garlic naan specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code found for butter naan or garlic naan. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for Indian flatbread subtypes.

preparation_method: brushed with butter or garlic butter after cooking

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of butter naan or garlic naan weighs approximately 90 g, roughly the size of a large oval flatbread served alongside a curry or dal.

nutrient_source.title: Naan, whole, commercially prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 18960) and ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods: Naan (plain, butter-brushed)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and secondary nutrients are drawn from USDA FoodData Central entry for commercially prepared naan (FDC ID 174924) cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for butter naan. Butter content adjustment applied: approximately 5 g butter per 90 g naan piece adds ~3.5 g fat and ~30 kcal per 100 g relative to plain naan. Garlic naan variant is nutritionally near-identical to butter naan with negligible garlic contribution per 100 g. Values represent a weighted composite of butter naan and garlic naan as served in Singapore Indian restaurants.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white wheat bread / naan GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for butter naan or garlic naan was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is assigned based on the published GI for white wheat flour naan (GI ~71, glucose reference) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database entry for naan bread made from refined wheat flour. Butter addition has negligible effect on GI per standard fat-addition studies. GL per serving calculated as: (71 × 46.9 g available carbohydrate per 90 g serving) / 100 = 33.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~430 mg per 100 g; ~387 mg per standard 90 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit butter naan to one piece per meal and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salty curries, as the combined sodium load can approach 600–800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high glycemic load per serving (GL ~33)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter naan and garlic naan are made from refined wheat flour (maida) and carry a high GI of approximately 71 and a glycemic load of ~33 per standard serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to half a piece, pair with high-fibre vegetables and protein-rich dal or legumes to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and consider whole-wheat roti as a lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~3.9 g per 100 g) and refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The butter used in butter naan and garlic naan contributes approximately 3.9 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to one piece per occasion and choose preparations with minimal added butter, as regular consumption of refined-flour, butter-enriched breads may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter naan and garlic naan are leavened Indian flatbreads made from refined wheat flour (maida), yogurt, butter, and a leavening agent such as yeast or baking powder. They are traditionally cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) at very high heat, producing a soft, slightly chewy interior with characteristic charred blisters on the surface. After cooking, the bread is generously brushed with melted butter; the garlic naan variant also receives a topping of minced garlic and sometimes fresh coriander. Butter roti is a closely related pan-cooked flatbread, also finished with butter, but typically thinner and less puffy than naan. In Singapore, these breads are staple accompaniments to North Indian curries, dal, and tandoori dishes and are widely available at Indian and Indian-fusion restaurants. Nutritionally, butter naan is energy-dense (~317 kcal per 100 g) with a moderate protein content (~8.7 g), meaningful fat from butter (~7.8 g, of which ~3.9 g is saturated), and a high carbohydrate load (~52 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is approximately 71 (high), making glycemic load per standard 90 g serving approximately 33, which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Sodium content (~430 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The refined flour base provides limited dietary fibre (~2.1 g per 100 g), and whole-wheat alternatives are preferable for metabolic health. Despite these considerations, butter naan and garlic naan are culturally important comfort foods and are best enjoyed in moderation as part of a balanced meal that includes vegetables, legumes, and lean protein.

ingredient_synonyms.maida: refined wheat flour, all-purpose flour, plain flour

confidence_reason: No exact Singapore-specific nutrient entry for butter naan or garlic naan was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN at the time of processing. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174924, commercially prepared naan) with a butter-content adjustment cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in the input JSON was listed as 'Mexican', which is incorrect; the food is Indian in origin and is served at Indian-Mexican fusion restaurants in Singapore. Cuisine corrected to 'Indian'. 2. The input listed two restaurants under the 'Three Amigos' brand, which operates as an Indian-Mexican fusion concept; naan is the Indian component of the menu. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entry for naan. 4. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — naan contains no vitamin C. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g from trace dairy trans fats in butter; confirmed trace per USDA source. 6. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat flour naan per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for butter naan was located. 7. Serving size of 90 g represents one standard restaurant-served piece of naan. 8. Butter roti is nutritionally treated as equivalent to butter naan for this composite record given near-identical ingredients and preparation; minor differences in thickness and leavening do not materially alter per-100 g macros. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174924 and are considered reliable estimates for this food category.
